title: Lab 3 · Evaluation of Measurement Uncertainty
summary: The heaviest part of the exam (**12 pts**). You build a complete **GUM uncertainty budget** in the mock Excel: classify each input as **Type A** or **Type B**, turn it into a standard uncertainty with the right divisor ($\sqrt3$, $\sqrt6$ or $k$), propagate variances through the model with sensitivity coefficients $u^2(y)=\sum c_j^2 u^2(x_j)$, find the effective degrees of freedom by **Welch–Satterthwaite**, read the coverage factor $k$ from **T.INV**, and report the expanded uncertainty $U=k\,u(y)$. The capstone reproduces the professor's *Uncertainty Table* example number-for-number.
objectives: Classify each input quantity as Type A (statistics on data) or Type B (other knowledge).
Convert a Type B input to a standard uncertainty with the correct divisor: rectangular $\sqrt3$, triangular $\sqrt6$, normal-with-coverage $k$.
Compute a Type A standard uncertainty $s$ (and $s/\sqrt n$ when an average is reported).
Find a sensitivity coefficient $c_j=\partial y/\partial x_j$ and the per-row contribution $u_j^2(y)=c_j^2 u^2(x_j)$.
Combine variances $u^2(y)=\sum_j u_j^2(y)$ and take the square root once.
Get the effective d.o.f. with Welch–Satterthwaite (and remember to floor it).
Read $k$ from T.INV$(1-\alpha/2,\nu_y)$ for the stated confidence and report $U=k\,u(y)$.

blocks.text: What the exam asks

You are given a measurand $y$ computed from several input quantities $x_j$ through a **mathematical model** $y=G(x_1,\dots,x_m)$ (a length corrected for temperature, a force $F=kx$, a pressure from a piston-cylinder, …). Each input is known only with some uncertainty. Your job is to fill the **uncertainty table** (the *uncertainty budget*) and report the result as $y\pm U$ at a stated confidence level. The marks are spread across every column of that table, so a tidy, complete budget is worth more than a fast guess.

blocks.title: Base units, always
blocks.text: The sheet says it in red: **NEVER USE MULTIPLES OR SUBMULTIPLES OF THE MEASUREMENT UNITS.** Convert µm→m, mg→kg, mW→W, mm/m→pure strain *before* you start. A budget done in mixed units gives a numerically wrong $u(y)$ even if every formula is right.
Type A vs Type B

Uncertainty obtained by **statistical analysis of repeated observations**. You compute a sample standard deviation $s$ (STDEV.S, with $n-1$ divisor). If the reported result is the **average** of $n$ readings, the standard uncertainty of that mean is $u=s/\sqrt n$; its degrees of freedom are $\nu=n-1$. Repeatability quoted as a measured standard deviation and reproducibility quoted as a measured variance are Type A in spirit.

Uncertainty obtained by **any other means**: a calibration certificate, an instrument resolution, a handbook coefficient, an estimated interval. You assume a probability distribution for the error and divide its half-width / expanded value by the matching divisor to get the standard uncertainty.

blocks.title: The divisor table — memorise it
blocks.text:
Given a Type B input, the standard uncertainty is the half-width (or expanded value) divided by:
- **Rectangular (uniform)**: $\;u=a/\sqrt3\;$ — used for **resolution** ($a=r/2$) and handbook/tabled coefficients.
- **Triangular**: $\;u=a/\sqrt6\;$ — when the centre is more likely than the edges.
- **Normal / Student with stated coverage**: $\;u=U/k\;$ — divide a certificate's *expanded* uncertainty by its coverage factor $k$ (recover $k$ from T.INV if only the confidence and d.o.f. are given).
- **A measured standard deviation** is *already* $u$ — divisor $1$.

blocks.caption: Type B variance: half-width squared over the distribution factor $k_d$ ($3$ rectangular, $6$ triangular, $k^2$ normal). The $n_d/n_r$ factor folds in how many measurements the value was determined on ($n_d$) vs. how many the final result averages ($n_r$).

blocks.text: Standard uncertainty in Excel

blocks.title: Exercise 4 — Type A from a sample, $s/\sqrt n$
Standard deviation vs variance vs expanded
blocks.text: Read the wording of every input carefully: a **standard deviation** is already $u$ (no divisor); a **variance** needs a square root ($u=\sqrt V$); an **expanded uncertainty** needs dividing by its coverage factor ($u=U/k$); a **maximum spread / range / max difference** is a half-width and takes a distribution divisor. Mixing these up is the single most common way to lose marks here.
Propagating to the output: sensitivity coefficients

Each standard uncertainty $u(x_j)$ must be translated into its effect on $y$. The **sensitivity coefficient** is the partial derivative of the model: $c_j=\partial G/\partial x_j$, evaluated at the nominal inputs. In the sheet it is computed numerically — perturb $x_j$ by a tiny $\Delta x_j$, see how much $y$ moves, and take $c_j=\Delta y_j/\Delta x_j$ — but for a simple product or ratio you can write it down by hand: for $y=kx$, $c_k=x$ and $c_x=k$; for $y=\prod x_i$, $c_j=y/x_j$.

blocks.caption: Law of propagation of variance (uncorrelated inputs). Each contribution is a sensitivity-squared times an input variance; the combined variance is their sum.

blocks.title: Why we square the sensitivity
blocks.text: Because variances add and a variance cannot be negative, only $c_j^2$ enters. A negative sensitivity (e.g. $c_\alpha=-1.60$ in the canonical sheet) contributes exactly as much as a positive one of the same magnitude. The sign matters only when you discuss *correlation* or *which way* an input pushes the result — never in the budget sum.
Effective degrees of freedom: Welch–Satterthwaite

The combined uncertainty $u(y)$ is a mix of contributions, each with its own degrees of freedom $
u_j$ (a Type A average has $
u=n-1$; a tabled rectangular value is conventionally given a large $
u$ such as 30 or 100; a regression slope has $
u=n-2$). To pick the right coverage factor we need a single **effective** number of degrees of freedom for $y$, given by the Welch–Satterthwaite formula.

blocks.caption: Effective degrees of freedom. The numerator is the combined variance squared; each term in the denominator is a contribution's variance squared over its own d.o.f. Take the FLOOR (Excel INT).

blocks.title: Floor it — never round up
blocks.text: The sheet uses **=INT(...)**, i.e. the floor. The raw Welch–Satterthwaite value in the canonical example is $\approx 15.7$ and becomes $\nu_y=15$, not 16. Rounding up would inflate the d.o.f., shrink $k$, and understate $U$ — a conservative budget always rounds the d.o.f. **down**.
Coverage factor and expanded uncertainty

For finite degrees of freedom the coverage factor is a **Student-$t$ quantile**, not the asymptotic normal $1.96$. For a two-sided confidence level $P$ (risk $\alpha=1-P$): $k=t_{1-\alpha/2,\,\nu_y}=$ **T.INV$(1-(1-P)/2,\ \nu_y)$**. In Excel `T.INV(p, df)` is the *left-tailed* inverse, so you pass the two-sided upper probability $1-\alpha/2$. (The legacy `TINV(α, df)` already does the two-sided split, so `TINV(1-P, ν)` gives the same number.)

blocks.tex: U(y)=k\cdot u(y),\qquad \text{report}\quad y\pm U \ \text{at confidence } P
blocks.caption: Expanded uncertainty: scale the combined standard uncertainty by the coverage factor. This is the half-width of the confidence interval reported with the result.

blocks.title: The Student-t distribution behind k
blocks.caption: Smaller degrees of freedom → fatter tails → larger coverage factor $k$. As $\nu\to\infty$ the t-quantile approaches the normal $z$ (e.g. $1.96$ at 95%).

blocks.text: The capstone: a full uncertainty budget

Everything now comes together in the professor's *Uncertainty Table* example. A length $L_T=800.074$ mm is measured with a flexometer and corrected to $20^\circ$C through $L_{20}=L_T[1+(\alpha-\beta)(20-T)]$. Six inputs enter the budget — instrument **bias** (Type B, expanded $3.7\,\mu$m at 95% on 4 measurements), reading **resolution** (Type B rectangular, $1\,\mu$m), **reproducibility** (variance $10.7\,\mu\text{m}^2$ over 10 replications, averaged over 5), the workpiece and instrument **temperature coefficients** $\alpha,\beta$ (rectangular, from handbook/maker) and the **temperature** $T=22\pm5^\circ$C. The table below lays one row per contribution and walks you through $u^2(x_j)\to u_j^2(y)\to u^2(y)\to\nu_y\to k\to U$.

blocks.title: Result of the canonical budget
blocks.text: $u^2(y)=1.3012\times10^{-11}\,\text{m}^2$, so $u(y)=3.607\,\mu$m. Welch–Satterthwaite gives $\nu_y=\lfloor15.7\rfloor=15$, hence $k=$T.INV$(0.975,15)=2.131$ and $U=k\,u(y)=7.69\,\mu$m. The corrected length is reported as $L_{20}=800.0748\ \text{mm}\pm7.7\,\mu$m at 95% confidence. The dominant contributions are the **bias** and the **reproducibility** — those are where you would spend money to improve the measurement.

blocks.title: Exercise 9 — capstone: the full Uncertainty Table

blocks.text: Two more full worked examples

The same scheme handles any model. Two further exam examples, reproduced with the source's cached numbers: a **strain-gauge rosette** where the bias is a *relative* standard deviation of each strain, and a **mercury-thermometer waiting time** $t=-(C_m/k_t)\ln(S_m/\Delta T)$ where the bias is given as an expanded uncertainty on 7 degrees of freedom.
More worked uncertainty examples

Three further **complete budgets** from the exam set, each laid out as a table with one row per contribution and reproduced number-for-number from the professor's solution sheets. They drill the full GUM workflow on richer models: a thread diameter measured with three wires, a metrological pressure gauge, and a force generated by a calibrated mass in the local gravity field.
- **Thread $d_2$** ($d_2=M-d(1+1/\sin(a/2))+p/(2\tan(a/2))$): watch the divisors — a *maximum spread* bias and a *half-range* pitch tolerance use different factors, and the pitch term ends up dominant.
- **Pressure gauge** ($P=m\,g(1-\rho_a/\rho_m)/(\pi((d+D)/4)^2)$): certificate biases on $\rho_m$ and $g$ must be turned into standard uncertainties with $s=U/k$ first; the **diameter reproducibility** dominates because the area sits squared in the denominator.
- **Force from gravity** ($F=m(g_{ass}+\Delta g+\gamma h)$): mixes an expanded-uncertainty mass bias, a reproducibility given as a *variance*, and a **regression slope** $\gamma$ with $\nu=n-2$ degrees of freedom.

blocks.title: Exercise 12 — thread diameter $d_2$ by three wires (full budget)
Exercise 14 — force from a calibrated mass & local gravity (full budget)
Exam checklist
blocks.text: 1. Convert every datum to **base units**. 2. For each input: Type A or B? which distribution? right divisor? 3. Build $u^2(x_j)$, including any $n_d/n_r$ factor. 4. Sensitivity $c_j$, then $u_j^2(y)=c_j^2 u^2(x_j)$. 5. $u^2(y)=\sum u_j^2(y)$, then $u(y)=\sqrt{\,\cdot\,}$. 6. $\nu_y$ by Welch–Satterthwaite, **floored**. 7. $k=$T.INV$(1-\alpha/2,\nu_y)$. 8. $U=k\,u(y)$; report $y\pm U$.

question.prompt: An instrument's **reading resolution** is $r$. What standard uncertainty does it contribute, and what is the underlying distribution?

options.content: $u=r$ — the resolution is itself the standard uncertainty

question.explanation: A reading can fall anywhere within one resolution step, so the error is **rectangular** with **half-width $a=r/2$**, giving $u=a/\sqrt3=(r/2)/\sqrt3$. **A** uses the full step as the half-width (double-counts). **C** picks the wrong divisor (triangular is for combined/peaked errors, not a flat resolution). **D** treats a half-width as a standard deviation — far too large.
question.theory: Type B rectangular input: standard uncertainty = half-width $/\sqrt3$. For a resolution the half-width is $r/2$.

practice.prompt: Five readings are repeated and the reported result is their **average**. The sample standard deviation is $s$. What is the standard uncertainty of the result, and which type is it?
practice.explanation: It is obtained statistically from data → **Type A**. Because the *average* is reported, its uncertainty is the standard deviation of the mean, $u=s/\sqrt n=s/\sqrt5$. **A** would be right only if a single reading (not the mean) were reported. **C** uses a Type B rectangular divisor — wrong, this input is statistical. **D** inflates instead of shrinking the uncertainty of the mean.
practice.theory: Type A for an average: $u=s/\sqrt n$ with $\nu=n-1$.

practice.prompt: In the law of propagation $u^2(y)=\sum_j c_j^2 u^2(x_j)$, one input has a **negative** sensitivity $c_j=-1.60$. How does it enter the combined variance?

options.content: It contributes $c_j^2\,u^2(x_j)>0$, exactly like a positive sensitivity of the same magnitude
It subtracts from the variance because $c_j<0$
It is dropped, since variances must be positive
Its sign flips the sign of $u(y)$

practice.explanation: Only $c_j^2$ enters, so the sign disappears: a $-1.60$ sensitivity adds the same $c_j^2 u^2(x_j)$ as $+1.60$. **B** misreads the formula — there is no subtraction for uncorrelated inputs. **C** would discard a real contribution. **D** is meaningless: $u(y)=\sqrt{u^2(y)}\ge0$ always.
practice.theory: For uncorrelated inputs every contribution $c_j^2 u^2(x_j)$ is non-negative; the sign of $c_j$ is irrelevant to the sum.

practice.prompt: A budget input is quoted as a **variance** $V=10.7\,\mu\text{m}^2$, and the reported result is the **average of $n_r=5$** measurements. What variance does this input contribute to $u^2(x_j)$?
practice.explanation: The variance of an average of $n_r$ measurements is the single-measurement variance divided by $n_r$: $V/n_r=10.7/5=2.14\,\mu\text{m}^2$ (matches UncTab $N40$). **A** ignores the averaging. **B** confuses a *standard deviation* with a *variance* (and a variance is what the column $u^2(x_j)$ holds). **D** multiplies instead of dividing — that would be the variance of a *sum*, not a mean.
practice.theory: Variance of the mean = variance / $n_r$. Keep variances (not std-devs) in the $u^2(x_j)$ column.
